questions.title: State management approach
questions.context: Backend is REST + WebSocket streaming prices every 5s. Need server state (orders, portfolio) + real-time state (prices). React Context built-in, Zustand lightweight, TanStack Query purpose-built for server state + caching.

options.label: React Context + useReducer
options.description: Zero dependencies, built-in React. Good for single-user app. More boilerplate for complex state. Fine for this scale.

options.label: TanStack Query (React Query)
options.description: Purpose-built for server state. Auto-caching, refetch, loading/error states built-in. Pair with minimal Zustand for real-time price state.

options.label: Zustand standalone
options.description: Tiny (1KB), simple API, no boilerplate. Works well but need to manually handle caching/refetch patterns.

questions.title: Routing library
questions.context: App needs 4-6 top-level routes: portfolio, orders, prices/assets, order detail. No nested layouts needed. React Router v6 is standard. TanStack Router has type-safe routes but heavier.

options.label: React Router v6
options.description: Industry standard, well-documented, simple enough for this app size. Loaders/actions via createBrowserRouter.

options.label: TanStack Router
options.description: Type-safe routes, built-in loaders, more complex setup. Overkill for 6 routes but good if app scales.

questions.title: API client approach
questions.context: Backend has ~12 REST endpoints + 1 WebSocket. OpenAPI docs auto-generated at /docs. Could generate typed client automatically.

options.label: Manual typed fetch with hooks
options.description: Simple. Write typed fetch wrappers per endpoint. Full control, minimal deps. Scales fine for 12 endpoints.

options.label: Axios + auto-generated types
options.description: Axios for HTTP, generate TS types from OpenAPI spec. Less manual typing, stays in sync with backend.

options.label: openapi-typescript codegen
options.description: Full client generation from OpenAPI spec. Zero manual API code. Magic but adds build step and generated files to manage.

questions.title: Navigation structure
questions.context: Single-user app with 4-5 sections: Dashboard/Portfolio, Orders, Assets/Prices, Trade History. Trading apps typically have dense persistent nav.

options.label: Left sidebar (collapsible)
options.description: Standard for trading dashboards. Fits many nav items. Collapsible to give charts more room later. Persistent visible nav.

options.label: Top nav bar
options.description: Simpler, good for fewer items (< 5). Works well on mobile. Less screen taken by nav. More common for general web apps.

options.label: Sidebar + top bar combination
options.description: Sidebar for primary nav, top bar for secondary actions (search, reset portfolio). Most flexible but more UI surface.

questions.title: Mobile navigation pattern
questions.context: Per CLAUDE.md: support 375px breakpoint. Must work on mobile during commute, monitoring positions.

options.label: Bottom tab bar
options.description: Thumbs-friendly on mobile. Persistent visible nav. 5-tab max with label. Very common in finance/mobile apps.

options.label: Hamburger drawer
options.description: Standard mobile pattern. Hides nav until tapped. More screen space for content. Extra tap to navigate.

options.label: Desktop sidebar collapses to icons on mobile
options.description: Sidebar shrinks to icon-only dock on tablet, bottom tabs on phone. Most adaptive but more CSS work.

questions.title: Color theme
questions.context: Trading apps traditionally dark mode. Light mode can cause eye strain during extended monitoring. Dark is default in most serious trading platforms.

options.label: Dark mode only
options.description: Serious trading aesthetic. Easier on eyes for long sessions. Less CSS to maintain. Matches industry standard (Bloomberg, TradingView, Robinhood dark).

options.label: Dark + Light with toggle
options.description: User choice. More dev time for theme system. Requires CSS variable architecture. Good accessibility practice. More CSS to maintain across phases.

questions.title: Portfolio page layout
questions.context: Backend returns: cash, positions value, total value, realized/unrealized P&L, win rate, avg gain/loss, max drawdown, positions list with current prices. Charts are Phase 3.

options.label: Summary cards row + positions table below
options.description: Clean hierarchy: key metrics in cards at top, detailed positions table below. Standard financial dashboard layout. Easy to scan.

options.label: Full dashboard with grid of metric cards
options.description: More visual. Grid of stat cards (cash, portfolio value, P&L, win rate, drawdown) in 2-3 columns. Positions table below. Feels more like a live dashboard.

options.label: Minimal: key numbers header + positions focus
options.description: Minimal. Just total value + P&L in header, then positions table takes main space. Chart placeholder area for Phase 3. Less visual noise.

questions.title: Portfolio empty state
questions.context: Initial state: 100,000 INR cash, zero positions, no trade history.

options.label: Show cash balance + 'No positions yet' message
options.description: Simple: show available cash prominently, empty state message with 'Place your first trade' CTA linking to orders page.

options.label: Show full layout with zeroes
options.description: Show the full portfolio layout with metric cards showing 0 values. Cash card shows 100K INR. Feels complete, not empty.

options.label: Onboarding empty state with suggestion
options.description: Friendly illustration. 'Your portfolio is ready. Start with 100,000 INR — try buying AAPL or BTC to see your first position.' Educational.

questions.title: Order placement UX
questions.context: Order form needs: symbol, side (BUY/SELL), order type (MARKET/LIMIT/STOP_LOSS/TAKE_PROFIT), quantity, optional price/stop_price.

options.label: Side panel / drawer
options.description: Slides in from right. Doesn't leave current page. User can see order list while filling form. Common in trading UIs.

options.label: Modal / dialog
options.description: Centered overlay. Simple, focused. Good for mobile. Blocks background until done. Less context of existing orders.

options.label: Dedicated order page with form
options.description: Full page for order placement. Most room for form + price preview + order validation. Clear full-screen focus. Requires navigation away.

questions.title: Order list display
questions.context: Orders have statuses: PENDING, OPEN, PARTIAL_FILLED, FILLED, CANCELLED, REJECTED. User needs to see key info at a glance and take action (cancel).

options.label: Table with status badges
options.description: Rows: symbol, side, qty, price, status badge, date, actions. Sortable columns. Most data-dense. Standard for trading.

options.label: Card list grouped by status
options.description: Grouped sections: Open Orders, Filled, Cancelled. Cards show key info. More visual, less dense. Better for scanning by status.

options.label: Tabs: Open vs History + table in each
options.description: Two tabs. 'Open Orders' shows cancellable orders in table. 'History' shows completed orders. Clean separation of active vs done.

questions.title: WebSocket reconnection strategy
questions.context: WebSocket at /ws/prices streams all asset prices every 5s. Connection drops happen. Single-user, no auth needed.

options.label: Auto-reconnect with exponential backoff
options.description: Reconnect automatically on disconnect. Backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, cap at 30s. Show 'Reconnecting...' banner. Seamless for user.

options.label: Auto-reconnect + fallback to polling
options.description: Auto-reconnect as above. If WS fails after N retries, fall back to polling GET /prices/{symbol} every 10s. Resume WS when connection restores.

options.label: Manual reconnect with status indicator
options.description: Show connection status indicator (green/red). User clicks to retry. Simpler but user must notice disconnection.

questions.title: Stale price visual indicator
questions.context: Backend marks prices as stale (is_stale: true) when provider fails but cached data exists. User should know price isn't current.

options.label: Yellow/orange warning badge next to stale price
options.description: Small warning icon + 'Stale' badge next to price. Color stands out against dark theme. Standard pattern.

options.label: Dim/desaturate stale prices visually
options.description: Stale prices shown with reduced opacity or gray tint. Subtle but noticeable. Less intrusive than badges.

options.label: Both: dimmed text + tooltip with last updated time
options.description: Price dimmed on hover shows 'Last updated: X seconds ago' tooltip. Balances visibility with information. Most informative.

questions.title: Data density for desktop
questions.context: Trading apps traditionally dense (show lots of data without scrolling). General web apps prefer spacious. Tradeoff: information density vs readability. Single-user = user knows their data.

options.label: Compact (trading-dense)
options.description: Smaller padding, more rows visible without scroll. Smaller font on data tables. Industry standard for trading (think Bloomberg terminal density).

options.label: Moderate (balanced)
options.description: Standard web app spacing. Comfortable row heights. Not as dense as trading terminals but not as airy as marketing sites. Readable at all sizes.

options.label: Spacious (modern web app)
options.description: Generous padding, larger fonts, lots of whitespace. Modern SaaS feel. Less data visible per screen but more readable. May need scrolling for many positions.

questions.title: Error and 404 pages
questions.context: App has 4-5 routes. Single-user so 404s rare from normal use. API errors (500, timeout) more likely.

options.label: Simple toast notifications for errors
options.description: API errors show as toast notifications (top-right). 404 redirects to portfolio. Minimal, keeps user in flow. Less surface area to maintain.

options.label: Inline error states per section
options.description: Failed API calls show inline error banner in the affected section (portfolio, orders). Retry button. 404 shows branded page with nav links.

options.label: Full error page with retry + status info
options.description: Dedicated error display with error details, retry button, connection status indicator. More dev work but thorough error handling.
